item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image could not be sourced and is assumed to be not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to like this, but it just doesn't hook me in.

Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually suffer from prosopagnosia, and the fact that the pictures change makes perfect sense to me. It doesn't matter who the pictures are of, because everyone is equally nondescript.

Ah, I'm really glad the description is fitting— prior to writing this, I heard a really fascinating personal story about prosopagnosia on RadioLab, from the perspective of two people in a relationship (one who experienced face-blindness, and one who did not). The theme of memory (especially the way we remember people after their deaths) is central to this and the other articles, so the "shuffling" effect wasn't just random.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It kind of reminds me of Eric and his things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually suffer from prosopagnosia, and the fact that the pictures change makes perfect sense to me. It doesn't matter who the pictures are of, because everyone is equally nondescript. If I open the book, look at the people in the pictures, close it, then open it again a while later and the people in the pictures have different faces, I wouldn't notice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea that there is a related set of anomalous objects. This isn't like a GOI, that intentionally produces multiple items. There is a mystery here. Did the family somehow get three SCP-worthy objects by random chance? Is the the region? (you know those ancient indian burial grounds do stuff like this all the time.) Is it the family itself? What became of them? I'm interested in learning more about them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I will just agree with this. Not everything needs to have an effect; it could just be an anomalous photo album.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cut a little more, but keeping the materials description. I like to distinguish what parts of the thing behave anomalously/are susceptible to damage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: fixed now— thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cut out some of the earlier description… you really needn't be that elaborate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: OK to change "Prosopagnosia" to lower case? Any reason it is capitalized here?

item.title: Re: Revision.
item.content:encoded: I agree with Reject. Original version had some flaws but wasn't bad, this rewrite is just the blandest.

item.title: Re: Revision.
item.content:encoded: I like it more than the original version, but still not enough to upvote.

item.title: Re: Revision.
item.content:encoded: shit you made it worse

item.title: Revision.
item.content:encoded: Completely changed the effect. My aim was to simplify it, instead of making it more elaborate— without losing the irrational weirdness. Feedback on changes (good, still bad, or shit you made it worse) appreciated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this has great potential to be scary and isn't weird enough to upvote on its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And the Gator is at +35. Again, I don't think you have to worry. :-P

Send me a PM when you're ready.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, SCP-091 is one of the best articles on the site. I simplified the effects of the photos in this one because they were getting over-complicated; the similarity to SCP-091 honestly didn't occur to me at all. I was mostly worried about the photography theme.

I don't think the "fake memories" concept itself is astoundingly singular, but if I can't make this particular effect more distinct then I'm going to transmute it to a Tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
D'oh… I meant SCP-1108. who would pick numbers so similar jeez

I'd definitely appreciate that. I think I'll take the material I have sandboxed and try it as a Tale draft first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are at least 2 other photo albums that I can think of, although I can't remember the numbers off-hand. There's also a pictoral history book (think like a Time-Life Retrospective coffee table book) with a similar effect where it rewrites the subject's memory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…1180 is currently above +50. I don't think we need to worry about that one going away any time soon.

I'd be willing to take a look at the swing & banjo if you like, though they might work better as a Tale about the group.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Funny, I sort of remember writing a similar article before. Maybe I've just read this one a few too many times and started to be affected by it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Isn't this already a thing? I remember reading about an anomalous photo album before…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am definitely not aiming to create a GOI; what I would like to pull of is a consistent little mythos for Research Sector-09 that centers around a few Old Florida families. They might work better as tales, though (the pessimistic part of me thinks that SCP-1180 is in a slow spiral toward deletion, and I'm trying to rewrite in Tale format).

The other artifacts are a tire swing and a banjo… existence pending my attempt to make this one more interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I cautiously and respectfully disagree. It sort of adds to the strangeness of the object. I like this one, though I can't quite put my finger on exactly what I like about it. It's quiet, it's subtle and it doesn't seem too overdone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Removing until I can make it sound better; the idea wouldn't be that it should be a total replacement, but that it would be more effective in some cases than wiping someone's brain entire.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, after a reread I think you're right about the tone.

I think I've emphasized the wrong elements, in that case. I'll try to make the description more concise and add back in the interview logs, so that the focus in on the actual CONTENT of the memories. I think because I wrote them all out and know what they are… Iiiiii underestimated how thin the article would be on interesting detail without them.

Glad that particular little element worked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It feels like I've seen similar articles before, and often enough that this just doesn't interest me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, but it seems to me that the weird stuff - the album rearranging itself - should be nixed. Just keep the memory altering effect. The other stuff seems to be weird just to be weird, and losing it might tighten up the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept just doesn't have enough going for it. The writing and execution are fine, but the idea does nothing for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something I find intriguing it "the other Bonifay artifacts". Now, I don't really want to see the Bonifays become another GOI, but the implication that there are multiple SCPs connected with them is interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am okay with it replacing the lives of D-class with its own imprinted life that never existed. Things like that really strike me in a particular fashion, though I'm vaguely aware that it is probably idiosyncratic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I could cut addendum two. When we already have the memory eraser drugs there's no reason to implement long term scp exposure to hundreds of people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Both. Also, compare this to SCP-091 and SCP-1500. The execution of the concept here doesn't make it stand out enough from those two. Nothing in this SCP really felt like I hadn't read it before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel that citation is overused here in some places. " "I don't remember doing that," was a frequent response to reminders of previously committed crimes or known family histories; equally frequent was the insistence, "you asked me here to talk about the family". " is the most egregious. It breaks the tone somewhat.

The rest of the article is not good enough for an upvote too, partly because we already have two skips with similar effects (1500 and 091), and partly because there is nothing interesting revealed about the Bonifay Family itself. Some hints about what they did and how they lived, and maybe how that caused the creation of 1890, would be a nice way to make the article more interesting. Maybe list the details consistent between all recollections.

The fact that the album have different number of pages, photos move, and there is no way to figure out which caption refers to who, is very neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Concept or presentation?

I'm sort of regretting overtly describing the effects in bullet points, instead of revealing it in logs/narratively.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
expressing perplexity if questioned closely about foodstuffs objects

Aside from that, this really, really, really does not grab me. I feel distinctly un-grabbed by this.

item.title: Note
item.content:encoded:
Addendum 1 used to feature interview logs that were almost as long as the article itself (which is already longer than planned); I removed them because I'd actually prefer to write one or two of them as Tales and maybe link them as supplements, if the SCP itself goes over well. in fact, it's theoretically one of three items that are thematically linked because I get ahead of myself like that

That said, I'm not sure if the idea really has a hook or even makes complete sense without the stories. So. Yeah. Let me know.
